Quick Facts
  • Son of Wali Webb and Malissa Lewis
  • Has three brothers, Andrew, Lamar and Michael
  • Competed in the 4x200-meter at the 2014 IAAF World Relays
  • Competed for Cerritos College in California before transferring to Texas A&M University
  • One of his favorite authors is Dr. Seuss.

  • 2013 NCAA Outdoor Championships, 1st

Quick Facts
  • Has four children with husband, Paul, whom she married in 2010. Uncle, Bob Webster, has worked with the U.S. Men's and Women's Hockey teams for more than 25 years Sister, Lisa Yost played collegiate volleyball for the University of Nebraska, Omaha, 1998-99, and the University of Tampa, 1999-2002
  • Husband Paul played collegiate volleyball for the University of Delaware, 1995-98
  • Began playing volleyball at age 5 at the YMCA
  • At age 11, was diagnosed with osteosarcoma (bone cancer) in her left tibia and later underwent a procedure known as rotationplasty to remove the cancerous bone, which included the knee
  • As part of the procedure, her lower leg was rotated 180 degrees and connected to the remnants of the femur to give her full range of movement and allow her to play sports
  • Played four years of high school volleyball, ran track for a year and also was on the diving team
  • Named "Best Blocker" of the Paralympic Games Rio 2016 with 19 blocks and 38 rebounds.
Paralympic Experience
  • 6-time Paralympian; 6-time Paralympic medalist (3 gold, 2 silver, 1 bronze)
    • Paralympic Games Paris 2024, gold (Team - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020, gold (Team - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, gold (Team - Women)
    • Paralympic Games London 2012, silver (Team - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Beijing 2008, silver (Team - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Athens 2004, bronze (Team - Women)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2022 – bronze (Team - Women)
  • Years of participation: Team - Women 2014, 2018, 2022
  • Medals: 3 (2 silver, 1 bronze)
    • Silver – 2018 (Team - Women); 2014 (Team - Women)
    • Bronze – 2022 (Team - Women)

bio.education: Cabot High School (Cabot, Ark.) ‘15  University of Arkansas ‘19, Chemistry
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Daughter of Brent and Amy Weeks
  • Has one twin sister, Tori, and three brothers, Tyler, Matthew and Connor
  • During her freshman season in 2016, became only the fifth woman in NCAA history to clear 15 feet and the youngest to accomplish the feat
  • Also became the first woman to sweep the NCAA indoor and outdoor pole vault titles in the same year in 2010
  • Trains with Team USA teammate, Sandi Morris. More
Career Highlights
  • 2016 NCAA Indoor Championships, 1st
Olympic Experience
  • 1-time Olympian
    • Olympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, 19th (Pole Vault - Women)

Quick Facts
  • In April 2008, one of Weggemann’s sisters took her to the University of Minnesota to watch the U.S. Team Trials for the Paralympic Games Beijing 2008 and was inspired to get back into swimming
  • A few months before, she received a series of epidural injections in her back to treat shingles
  • After the third she lost movement from the waist down
  • Daughter of Ann and Chris Weggemann and has two older sisters, Christin and Jessica, and one brother-in-law, Sean
  • Hobbies include traveling, scuba diving, spending time with friends and family, and enjoying the outdoors and athletics
  • Led delegates in the Pledge of Allegiance at the 2016 Democratic National Convention
  • Won the 2011 Best Female Athlete with a Disability ESPY Award
  • Three-time Disabled Athlete of the Year by USA Swimming (2009, 2010, 2011)
  • Named the Female Disabled Swimmer of the Year of 2009 and 2010 by Swimming World Magazine
  • She was a part of SwimSwam.com's broadcast team during the 2014 Pan Pacific Championships
  • She suffered an arm injury in March 2014, which stopped her from competing at the 2014 Pan Pacific Championships
  • She is a motivational speaker for The Factory Agency
Paralympic Experience
  • 4-time Paralympian; 7-time Paralympic medalist (4 gold, 2 silver, 1 bronze)
    • Paralympic Games Paris 2024, gold (200m Individual Medley SM7 - Women), silver (50m Butterfly S7 - Women), 8th (50m Freestyle S8 - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020, gold (100m Backstroke S7 - Women, 200m Individual Medley SM7 - Women), silver (50m Butterfly S7 - Women), 5th (100m Freestyle S7 - Women), 7th (50m Freestyle S8 - Women), 9th (100m Breaststroke SB6 - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, 5th (100m Breaststroke SB7 - Women, 200m Individual Medley SM8 - Women), 7th (100m Butterfly S8 - Women), 8th (100m Freestyle S8 - Women), 9th (100m Backstroke S8 - Women), 11th (50m Freestyle S8 - Women), 12th (400m Freestyle S8 - Women)
    • Paralympic Games London 2012, gold (50m Freestyle S8 - Women), bronze (4 x 100m Medley Relay 34 Points - Women), 4th (100m Freestyle S8 - Women), 6th (100m Breaststroke SB7 - Women, 200m Individual Medley SM8 - Women), 7th (100m Backstroke S8 - Women)
World Championships Experience

Quick Facts
After playing soccer his freshman year, Weise had a traumatic brain injury, following shortly after by a second one, and he thought soccer would no longer be a part of his life. That was until he was contacted by the Paralympic coaching staff. Since then, he’s become a member of the national team and made his first appearance with the team at the Footie 7 Tournament in Portugal in March 2015.

Playing Career:

  • Early Career
    Began playing field hockey at school at age 12
     
  • International Highlights
    2002 -     USA National Team...USA/Chile Series...2003 - Named to the U.S. National Team...Pan American Cup Qualifier Team (Santo Domingo/2nd)...USA Eagles Cal Cup Champions...Pan American Games (Santo Domingo)...U.S. Pan American Games team (5th/Santo Domingo)...2004 - U.S. National Team...Pan American Cup (7th, London, Ontario)...2005 - U.S. National Squad...2006 - U.S. National Squad...Canada Test Series (Chula Vista, Calif)...Named to 2006-07 USA National Squad at USA National Championships (Chula Vista, Calif.)...USA vs. Trinidad & Tobago Test Series (Virginia Beach, Va.)...2007 -USA vs. Mexico Pan American Games Qualifying Series (USA wins series 2-0-1)...USA vs. Trinidad & Tobago tour (Port of Spain, TRI; USA wins series 4-0)...China 4 Nation Tournament Team (4th, CHN, JPN, EGY, USA - Lanzhou City, CHN)...XV Pan American Games Team (Rio de Janeiro, 7th)...2008 - Olympic Qualifying Tournament (Auckland, NZL; 6th_
     
  • National
    2006 -     USA National Championships (Chula Visa, Calif.)...2007 - Placed 4th at USA National Championships with San Francisco Bay Area High Performance Center (Chula Vista, Calif.)...Named "Goalkeeper of the Tournament" for the National Championships.

Quick Facts


Career High: Captaining my country's team
Career Low: Missing out on the 2003 Pan Am Games semi-finals on goal difference despite beating the Dominican Republic 23-0.
Sport ambition: Medal at Pan Am Games
Ambition after hockey: Continue my legal career
Other than field hockey, my favorite Olympic sport is : Track & field
Most influential person in your sporting career: Alan 'Sinnie' Sinclair. He is one of the most innovative, knowledgeable and perceptive coaches I've ever worked with. He developed my technique and had a real talent for getting the best out of me and my team mates. A knack for teaching players without them realising they were learning. Probably a man slightly ahead of his time.
Before every game I : Eat Jaffa Cakes
Most unusual thing that has happened to you: An Aussie fan asked to take a picture of me holding her baby son!
I can't live without: my laptop
Most people don't know that I: speak German
My philosophy on life: Never be afraid of moving slowly only of standing still.
Favorites: Food: Chicken Tikka Badami (Curry); Movie: Anchorman; Musical Artist: Depends on my mood;
 

Major International Competitions:
